participants.reasoning: The extracted text matches the official inclusion criteria and randomized/completing sample sizes, with only minor recruitment-source and baseline detail differences.
participants.extracted: Adults aged 35 to 65 years with physician-diagnosed type 2 diabetes mellitus for at least 1 year, body mass index of 27.0 or higher, and glycosylated hemoglobin of 7.0% or higher who were not using insulin. Participants were recruited from local medical practices and community advertising; the randomized sample was 58 adults, with 32 assigned to MB-EAT-D and 36 to Smart Choices, and 52 completing the study (27 and 25, respectively).
participants.official:
Recruited through local medical practices, the university newswire, radio and electronic advertisements, and community flyers. Dates of recruitment not reported.
Inclusion criteria:
Eligibility criteria for participation included being age 35 to 65 years with physician‐diagnosed T2DM for ≥ 1 year, body mass index ≥ 27.0, glycosylated haemoglobin ≥ 7.0%, and not requiring insulin therapy.
Exclusion criteria:

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official interventions in structure, duration, session format, core content, and the distinction between MB-EAT-D and Smart Choices, with only minor wording differences.
interventions.extracted: The trial compared two manualized, group-based 3-month diabetes education interventions delivered in 8 weekly and 2 biweekly 2.5-hour sessions, plus 1- and 3-month follow-up sessions. The first intervention was MB-EAT-D (Mindfulness-Based Eating Awareness Training for Diabetes), a mindfulness-based program adapted for adults with type 2 diabetes that focused on mindful eating, guided meditation, awareness of physical versus emotional hunger, recognition of eating triggers, and cultivating nonjudgmental awareness of food-related thoughts and sensations. It also included basic information on diet, physical activity, weight regulation, and glycemia, but did not set specific diet or activity goals; participants received CDs and were encouraged to meditate regularly and practice brief mindfulness exercises. The second intervention, Smart Choices, was a behavioral diabetes self-management education program based on social cognitive theory and meaningful learning that provided detailed instruction on carbohydrate and fat intake, blood glucose, lipids, weight control, and self-efficacy building through goal setting, hands-on activities, and problem solving, with calorie, carbohydrate, and total fat goals assigned. Both interventions included follow-up maintenance sessions; the MB-EAT-D follow-ups reinforced meditation practice, whereas Smart Choices follow-ups included walking.
interventions.official:
Intervention(duration 12 weeks, setting not reported):
MB‐EAT for Diabetes (MB‐EAT‐D) is a variation of the intervention developed originally for binge‐eating disorder and obesity (Kristeller 2011). Mindful eating is a tool to cultivate attention to increase non‐judgmental awareness of internal experience and automatic patterns related to eating. The training is designed to help individuals interrupt “mindless” and stress‐related eating and re‐engage the natural physiological processes of eating

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include many measures and multiple assessment timepoints, whereas the official outcomes list only anxiety, depression, weight, and self-efficacy at 24 weeks, so they do not match.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure body weight (kg) using an electronic scale at baseline, immediately postintervention, and at 1-month and 3-month follow-up; dietary intake using the 110-item Block 2005 Food Frequency Questionnaire, including total energy intake (kcal) and servings per 1,000 kcal of vegetables, fruits and fruit juices, grains, and meat/fish/poultry/eggs at baseline, immediately postintervention, and follow-up; diabetes and nutrition knowledge using a multiple-choice knowledge instrument at baseline, immediately postintervention, and follow-up; nutrition outcome expectations using an 11-point outcome expectations inventory at baseline, immediately postintervention, and follow-up; nutrition self-efficacy regarding promoters of diabetes self-management and barriers to diabetes self-management using an 11-point diabetes-specific nutrition self-efficacy questionnaire at baseline, immediately postintervention, and follow-up; eating self-efficacy using the 25-item Eating Self-Efficacy Scale at baseline, immediately postintervention, and follow-up; eating behavior/cognitive restraint, disinhibition of control, and hunger susceptibility using the Three-Factor Eating Questionnaire at baseline, immediately postintervention, and follow-up; anxiety using the 21-item Beck Anxiety Inventory at baseline, immediately postintervention, and follow-up; depression using the 21-item Beck Depression Inventory-II at baseline, immediately postintervention, and follow-up; and mindfulness using the Five-Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire subscales (nonreactivity, observing, acting with awareness, describing, and nonjudging) at baseline, immediately postintervention, and follow-up.
outcomes.official: Follow‐up at 24 weeks: anxiety, depression, weight, self‐efficacy
